coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doping to enhance the separation selectivity of glucose-based carbon dots-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Ning
Chen, Jia
Zhou, Hang
Chand Ali, Mohammad
Guan, Ming
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: Doping is often used to alter or improve the properties of nanomaterials. In this work, a novel glucose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Glc-NCDs)-bonded silica stationary phase (Sil-Glc-NCDs) was synthesized and characterized carefully. Glc-NCDs were prepared using glucose and aspartic acid as carbon and nitrogen sources, and then grafted on silica surface via isocyanatopropyl as linker using deep eutectic solvents as reaction medium. The synthesis was confirmed by elemental analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and microscopic imaging techniques. And then Sil-Glc-NCDs were packed in the stainless columns for hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC). Due to the synergistic effect between the functional groups of doped carbon dots, Sil-Glc-NCDs column showed enhanced separation selectivity compared with previous non-doped Sil-Glc-CDs column. Sil-Glc-NCDs column can be used for separation of base, nucleosides and antibiotics etc. This column was successfully applied to determine the content of roxithromycin in the capsule, which was found that the concentration was 2.45 mg/mL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Deep eutectic solvents
Stationary phase
Hydrophilic interaction chromatography
Roxithromycin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic procedure of Glc-NCDs and Sil-Glc-NCDs.
TEM image of (a) Glc-NCDs, (b) Sil-Glc-NCDs, (c) confocal fluorescence images of Sil-Glc-NCDs.

Effect of (a) acetonitrile concentration, (b) salt concentration, (c) pH values, (d) column temperature on the retention factor (k) of nucleosides and bases on the Sil-Glc-CDs. Analytes: (1) thymine, (2) thymidine, (3) uridine, (4) xanthine, (5) adenine nucleoside, (6) adenine, (7) cytosine, Chromatographic conditions: (a) 75%–95% acetonitrile, aqueous ammonium acetate (50 mM, pH = 6.8), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (b) 95% acetonitrile, 20–200 mM aqueous ammonium acetate (pH = 6.8), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (c) 95% acetonitrile, aqueous ammonium acetate (50 mM, pH = 3.2–7.0), column temperature is 25 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1; (d) 95% acetonitrile, aqueous ammonium acetate (50 mM, pH = 6.8), column temperature is 10–40 °C, flow rate 1.0 mL min−1.

Separation of (a) amino acids, (b) saccharides, (c) ginsenosides and (d) antibiotics on Sil-Glc-NCDs. Solutes: (a) (1) Glutamine, (2) Tryptophan, (3) Leucine, (4) Valine, (5) Threonine, (6) Alanine, (7) Proline, (8) Histidine, mobile phase: 85% acetonitrile,15% aqueous ammonium acetate solution (50 mM), column temperature 30 °C, flow rate 1 mL min−1; (b) (1) rhamnose, (2) arabic, (3) fructose, (4) galactose, (5) sucrose, (6) maltose, (7) lactose, mobile phase: 90% acetonitrile,10% aqueous ammonium acetate solution (50 mM), column temperature 30 °C, flow rate 1 mL min−1; (c) (1) Rh1, (2) Rg2, (3) Rg1, (4) Rd, (5) Rc, (6) Rb3, (7) Rb2, (8) Rb1, mobile phase is the same as (a); (d) (1) thiamphenicol, (2) enrofloxacin, (3) fleroxacin, (4) roxithromycin, (5) erythromycin, (6) norfloxacin, (7) tetracycline, mobile phase is the same as (a).
Separation of (a) amino acids and (b) ginsenosides on Sil-Glc-NCDs, Sil-Glc-CDs and two commercial columns, mobile phase is the same as Fig. 4a.

The repeatability test of Sil-Glc-NCDs column, (1) thymine, (2) uridine, (3) adenine nucleoside, (4) cytosine, (5) thymidine, (6) xanthine, (7) adenine; chromatographic condition: 95% acetonitrile, 5% aqueous ammonium acetate solution (50 mM), column temperature 25 °C, flow rate 1 mL min−1.
(a) Standard curve of roxithromycin; (b) Chromatogram of the separation of roxithromycin standard and actual sample, chromatographic conditions are the same as Fig. 4a.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doping to enhance the separation selectivity of glucose-based carbon dots-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doping is often used to alter or improve the properties of nanomaterials. In this work, a novel glucose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Glc-NCDs)-bonded silica stationary phase (Sil-Glc-NCDs) was synthesized and characterized carefully. Glc-NCDs were prepared using glucose and aspartic acid as carbon and nitrogen sources, and then grafted on silica surface via isocyanatopropyl as linker using deep eutectic solvents as reaction medium. The synthesis was confirmed by elemental analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and microscopic imaging techniques. And then Sil-Glc-NCDs were packed in the stainless columns for hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC). Due to the synergistic effect between the functional groups of doped carbon dots, Sil-Glc-NCDs column showed enhanced separation selectivity compared with previous non-doped Sil-Glc-CDs column. Sil-Glc-NCDs column can be used for separation of base, nucleosides and antibiotics etc. This column was successfully applied to determine the content of roxithromycin in the capsule, which was found that the concentration was 2.45 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of biological and medical science, the separation of middle and strong polar compounds is becoming more and more important [1,2]. However, the traditional chromatographic models, such as reversed-phase and normal phase chromatography, do not achieve satisfactory results in the separation of these compounds, which often requires derivatization or other ways [3,4]. In 1990, hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC) emerged by Alpert [5]. The stationary phase is composed of polar substances, and the mobile phase is composed of organic solvent and water (usually containing buffer salt), which becomes an alternative strategy to separate polar and hydrophilic compounds [6], such as amino acids [7,8], nucleosides [9,10]. In recent years, it is more and more popular to be applied to the separation of proteomics and metabonomics [11–15].

The composition of chromatographic stationary phase and mobile phase mainly determines the retention of analytes [16–18]. Especially, stationary phase is the key to separation. This various organic and inorganic materials were used to modify silica gel for HILIC separation. In addition to the familiar amino, amide, diol and cyano groups, various functional groups such as ionic liquids, saccharides and hydrophilic polymers were also used to modify silica and used as HILIC stationary phases [19–24]. Recently, carbon dots as one kind of carbonaceous nanomaterials with small size and rich groups, were also grafted on the silica surface, which were proved as a new kind of HILIC stationary phases with good performance [25–27].

Glucose, as the most commonly used unit of carbohydrate, is abundant in nature. It can be pyrolyzed into special fluorescent carbon quantum dots in the presence of phosphate or ammonium hydroxide [28–32]. In our previous work, it was confirmed that glucose carbon dots can be easily grafted on silica surface and then used as a new HILIC stationary phase (Sil-Glc-CDs) [33]. However, how to improve the separation selectivity is still a challenge.

In this paper, in order to further improve the separation selectivity, nitrogen is introduced to prepare glucose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Glc-NCDs) by pyrolyzing glucose and aspartic acid together. It is expected that the rich amino functional groups of aspartic acid will provide better hydrophilic chromatographic selectivity for Glc-NCDs. Thus Glc-NCDs was grafted on 3-isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane-modified silica in deep eutectic solvents (DES). And the obtained Sil-Glc-NCDs column really has enhanced selectivity when compared with Sil-Glc-CDs column at the separation of polar compounds in HILIC. In addition, the column can also be used for the quantitative determination of roxithromycin in roxithromycin capsules. Compared with the reported literatures [34–36], it has the advantages of simple operation and high repeatability.

Spherical silica gel (particle size 5.0–5.5 μm, pore diameter: 100 Å, specific surface area: 306 m2/g) was purchased from Fuji Silicon Chemical Co., Ltd. (Aichi, Japan). 3-Isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane (IPTS, 95%) was purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). d-Glucose, l-aspartic acid, triethylenediamine (DABCO), sodium hydroxide and choline chloride (ChCl) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Toluene, ethylene glycol (EG), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, absolute ethanol, and methanol were purchased from Cologne Chemical (Sichuan, China). All chemical standards (nucleosides and base, antibiotics, ginsenosides, amino acids, saccharides) used for chromatographic separation were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Agilent 1260 Infinity LC (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to evaluate column performance, including diode array detector (Agilent Technologies, USA) and Alltech 3300 Evaporative Light Scattering Detector (ELSD) with GCK 3302 air source were used for detection of analyte without UV absorption. The referenced XBridge HILIC and the commercial Globalsil™ amino columns were purchased from Waters Corporation of the United States and Suzhou Global Chromatography Co., Ltd. (5 μm, 150 × 4.6 mm i. d.), respectively. The infrared spectrum results were obtained by IFS 120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Bruker, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy and scanning electron microscope images were obtained from Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI USA) and JSM-6701F scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan), respectively; Elemental analysis results were measured by the Vario ELIII Elemental Analyzer (EA, Elementar, Germany). The FV1200 laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, Olympus, Japan) provides laser confocal microscopy images.

Glc-NCDs were prepared by high temperature pyrolysis of glucose and aspartic acid [37]. The specific steps are as follows: d-glucose and l-aspartic acid (molar ratio, 7:3) were fully dissolved in a beaker containing 10 mL of water, the pH of the mixed solution to 8.0 was adjusted with 1.0 mol L−1 aqueous NaOH. The beaker was then placed in an oven at 125 °C for 30 min and the reaction was continued at 200 °C for 20 min. Then the crude product was re-dissolved with 20 mL of water after the reaction finished, and the solution was placed in a dialysis bag with a molecular interception of 3000 WD, dialyzed for two days, and finally freeze-dried to obtain Glc-NCDs.

Considering that Glc-NCDs have poor solubility in conventional solvents, and the bonding reaction should avoid the existence of water. The low-viscous and facile obtained DES was selected as the reaction solvents. The preparation method of this DES is very simple. In short, ChCl and EG are mixed at a molar ratio of 1:3, and then magnetically stirred in an oil bath at 80 °C until a uniform clear solution is formed.

The preparation of Sil-Glc-NCDs (Fig. 1 ) was similar to the previously reported Sil-Glc-CDs [33]. Briefly, 3.0 g of silica gel was completely dispersed in 20 mL of anhydrous toluene solution, 6 mM of IPTS was added, and reacted at 120 °C. After 24 h, the prepared isocyanatopropyl-modified silica (Sil-IPTS) was washed successively with anhydrous toluene and anhydrous DMF. After drying, Sil-IPTS was ultrasonically dispersed in 25 mL of DES, and 0.8 g Glc-NCDs was added. Under nitrogen atmosphere the reaction was maintained at 65 °C for 24 h. The prepared Sil-Glc-NCDs stationary phase was then washed several times with water, methanol, ethanol, acetone, and finally dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

2.0 g of Sil-Glc-NCDs was ultrasonically dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water to prepare a homogeneous slurry. The methanol was used as a displacement solution, and the slurry was packed into the stainless column (150 mm × 4.6 mm, i. d.) under a pressure of 40 MPa.

The content of roxithromycin in the capsules is determined using our new column. The specific process is as follows: 8.3 mg/mL of roxithromycin stock solution was prepared and diluted into several solutions with equal concentration gradient, then injected for analysis and record the peak area. Unknown concentration roxithromycin capsule solution was prepared and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane before analysis.

The morphology of Glc-NCDs and Sil-Glc-NCDs were characterized by TEM and SEM. Many Glc-NCDs particles can be observed in Fig. 2 a, the diameter of Glc-NCDs is 2.45 ± 0.51 nm (Fig. S1), Fig. 2b shows that Glc-NCDs are uniformly coated on the surface of spherical silica, and the thickness of Glc-NCDs is about 12 nm. SEM images also indicates that the surface of spherical silica is evenly wrapped by Glc-NCDs (Fig. S2).

FT-IR was further used to characterize the bonding of Glc-NCDs on the surface of spherical silica as displayed in Fig. S3. First, the Glc-NCDs spectrum has a characteristic absorption peak at 3386 cm−1, mainly from the stretching vibration peaks of OH and NH, it can be observed the stretching vibration peak of CO and the bending vibration peak of NH at 1610 cm−11387 cm−1 represents the characteristic peak of CC, and the strong absorption peak at 1067 cm−1 is due to the oxidation of some Glc-NCDs. The characteristic absorption peak of the hydrophilic functional group brought by Glc-NCDs can also be observed in the Sil-Glc-NCDs spectrum, 1550 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of secondary amine and Si–O on the silica gel or silane reagent can be found at stretching vibration peak 1094 cm−1.

The results of elemental analysis were listed in Table 1 . Compared with the C and N contents of Sil-IPTS (4.72% and 0.89%), the C and N percentages of Sil-Glc-NCDs are 8.56% and 1.44%, respectively. The obvious increasing of the contents of C and N confirmed the successful preparation of the stationary phase. In addition, the data of Glc-CDs and Sil-Glc-CDs were listed in Table 1.

In order to understand the retention mechanism of Sil-Glc-NCDs stationary phase, the effects of acetonitrile content, buffer salt concentration, buffered pH and column temperature on the retention of nucleosides and bases were investigated. First, acetonitrile content was adjusted in the range of 75%–95%, the relationship between acetonitrile content and analyte retention time was examined, the results are given in Fig. 3 a. The results show that the logarithm of the retention factor increases with the increase of acetonitrile content, which is a typical HILIC feature.

Adding a buffer salt to the mobile phase can effectively inhibit electrostatic interaction between the stationary phase and the analyte, improve peak shape and selectivity. As shown in Figs. 3b, 10–200 mM ammonium acetate in the mobile phase were selected, and the results showed that the retention of all nucleoside and base increased with the increasing of buffer salt concentration.

The effect of buffered salt pH on the retention of analyte is achieved by altering the ionization state of the analyte and stationary phase, so adjusting the pH in the range of 2.8–6.8 investigates the effect of buffered salt pH on retention (see Fig. 3c). In general, except for xanthine, the retention time of other nucleoside and base increases with increasing pH, especially when the acidity is strong, which is due to the protonation between the analyte and the stationary phase. However, the abnormality of xanthine may be due to its uncharged structure, the effect of buffer pH on retention is not obvious.

The effect of column temperature on retention can be explored by the Van der Hoff equation, and the column temperature is in the range of 15–55 °C (Fig. 3d). The relationship between lnk and 1/T of all analytes showed a linear correlation with a correlation coefficient between 0.93 and 0.99, indicating that hydrophilic partitioning plays a dominant role in material retention. The Van der Hoff equation parameters are shown in Table S1, combined with the Gibbs free energy formula, the ΔHо of all solutes is negative, indicating that the migration process of the solute from the mobile phase to the stationary phase is an exothermic process, low temperature is conducive to solute retention. The ΔHо of all analytes is greater than TΔSо, indicating that the morphological effect of solute is more obvious in the transfer of solute from the mobile phase to the stationary phase [38,39].

Ginsenosides, amino acids, antibiotics and saccharides are typical polar compounds to explore the selectivity of the stationary phase in HILIC mode [40–44]. As shown in Fig. 4 a–d, separation of eight amino acids, eight ginsenosides, seven saccharides and seven antibiotic compounds all showed good performance of the Sil-Glc-NCDs stationary phase, suggesting the column has excellent hydrophilic properties. The values of asymmetry factor (As), theoretical plate number (N/m) and resolution (Rs) of each compound are listed in Table S2-4. Eight kinds of ginsenosides can be completely separated within 10 min, and the resolution is better. In addition, the column exhibits good column efficiency, taking the peak of tryptophan as an example, the column efficiency is up to 68,000 plates/m.

In addition, in order to further explore the effect of nitrogen-doped column (Sil-Glc-NCDs) on the separation selectivity of the column, two commercial columns (XBridge HILIC and Globalsil™ Amide) and non-doped column (Sil-Glc-CDs) was used as references. After compared the chromatograms obtained from different columns as illustrated in Fig. 5 a–b and Fig. S4a-c, there is a significant difference in the retention time of the analyte on each stationary phase. First, almost all compounds have the longest retention times on the Globalsil™ amino column; second, the XBridge HILIC column and the Sil-Glc-CDs column are relatively short; third, under the same chromatographic conditions, the peak order of all the analytes on the two columns of Sil-Glc-CDs and Sil-Glc-NCDs was exactly the same, and the retention time and separation effect were quite different. It shows that the interaction between the two stationary phases and the analyte is the same, and the difference of separation effect may be due to the synergistic effect between the functional groups of the precursor, which promotes the separation of the compounds, so that the doped stationary phase has better separation selectivity and chromatographic column efficiency.

Intra-column stability was investigated using nucleosides and base as probes and inject 10 times under the same chromatographic conditions as displayed in Fig. 6 , the RSD (n = 10) values of the retention factors of the analytes was between 0.32 and 0.97. The inter-column repeatability of this stationary phase was also proved to be good. Two batch columns were prepared by the same methods and injected under the same chromatographic conditions, chromatogram and elemental analysis results are shown in Fig. S5 and Table S5, indicating that the stationary phase can be repeatedly prepared.

The standard solution of roxithromycin was accurately configured in the concentration range of 0.4–3.3 mg/mL, and the standard curve was obtained as shown in Fig. 7 a. The linear regression equation of roxithromycin is shown in (1), and the correlation coefficients (R2) are 0.9991. The Sil-Glc-NCDs stationary phase is used to separate and quantify roxithromycin from roxithromycin capsule solution (see Fig. 7b). The concentration of roxithromycin was found to be 2.45 mg/mL.
(1) y = 1418.39x-356.07

In this paper, a glucose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots-functionalized silica stationary phase was prepared and evaluated in HILIC with good separation performance for polar and hydrophilic analytes. Compared with two commercial columns and the previous reported column packed with non-doped glucose-based carbon dots-grafted silica, our new column shows satisfactory separation effect with higher selectivity. Enhanced selectivity can be attributed that the doped carbon dots prepared by different precursors having rich functional groups, which show synergistic effect in chromatographic separation, and the generation of synergistic effect increases the interaction sites with analytes. Furthermore, the application of this column in determining the content of roxithromycin in capsules reveal that it may be applied for other hydrophilic complex samples.

Ning Yuan: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jia Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Hang Zhou: Software, Validation. Mohammad Chand Ali: Investigation. Ming Guan: Methodology. Hongdeng Qiu: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21822407), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFD1002403) and Xinjiang “Tianshan Youth Plan” Project (No. 2017Q027).

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in separation science applied to metabonomics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, L. Cristina, Advances in separation science applied to metabonomics, Electrophoresis 29 (2008) 3724-3736.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New advances in separation science for metabolomics: resolving chemical diversity in a post-genomic era

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Kuehnbaum, B. Philip, New advances in separation science for metabolomics: resolving chemical diversity in a post-genomic era, Chem. Rev. 113 (2013) 2437-2468.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The use of reverse-phase columns for separation of unsubstituted carbohydrates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Gary, P. Shawn, L. James, The use of reverse-phase columns for separation of unsubstituted carbohydrates, J. Carbohyd. Chem. 5 (1986) 83-97.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino acid analysis by reverse-phase high-performance liquid chromatography: precolumn derivatization with phenylisothiocyanate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Heinrikson, S. Meredith, Amino acid analysis by reverse-phase high-performance liquid chromatography: precolumn derivatization with phenylisothiocyanate, Anal. Biochem. 136 (1984) 65-74.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophilic-interaction chromatography for the separation of peptides, nucleic acids and other polar compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Alpert, Hydrophilic-interaction chromatography for the separation of peptides, nucleic acids and other polar compounds, J. Chromatogr. A 499 (1990) 177-196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in development and characterization of stationary phases for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Qiao, X. Shi, G. Xu, Recent advances in development and characterization of stationary phases for hydrophilic interaction chromatography, TrAC Trends. Anal. Chem. 81 (2016) 23-33.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and characterization of carbon dot-decorated silica stationary phase in deep eutectic solvents for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, X. Qiao, T. Cai, J. Chen, Z. Li, H. Qiu, Preparation and characterization of carbon dot-decorated silica stationary phase in deep eutectic solvents for hydrophilic interaction chromatography, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 409 (2017) 2401-2410.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A phenylenediamine-based carbon dot-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, H. Zhang, J. Chen, Z. Li, L. Zhao, H. Qiu, A phenylenediamine-based carbon dot-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography, Analyst 145 (2020) 1056-1061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots and their precursor co-immobilized on silica for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Cai, H. Zhang, J. Chen, Z. Li, H. Qiu, Polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots and their precursor co-immobilized on silica for hydrophilic interaction chromatography, J. Chromatogr. A 1597 (2019) 142-148.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two copolymer-grafted silica stationary phases prepared by surface thiol-ene click reaction in deep eutectic solvents for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hu, T. Cai, H. Zhang, J. Chen, Z. Li, L. Zhao, Z. Li, H. Qiu, Two copolymer-grafted silica stationary phases prepared by surface thiol-ene click reaction in deep eutectic solvents for hydrophilic interaction chromatography, J. Chromatogr. A 1609 (2020) 460446.
J.C. Linden, C.L. Lawhead, Liquid chromatography of saccharides, J. Chromatogr. A 105 (1975) 125-133.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Accurate and efficient amino acid analysis for protein quantification using hydrophilic interaction chromatography coupled tandem mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kambhampati, J. Li, B.S. Evans, D.K. Allen, Accurate and efficient amino acid analysis for protein quantification using hydrophilic interaction chromatography coupled tandem mass spectrometry, Plant. Methods 15 (2019) 46.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mixed-mode liquid chromatography with a stationary phase co-functionalized with ionic liquid embedded C18 and an aryl sulfonate group

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ren, K. Zhang, D. Gao, Q. Fu, J. Zeng, D. Zhou, L. Wang, Z. Xia, Mixed-mode liquid chromatography with a stationary phase co-functionalized with ionic liquid embedded C18 and an aryl sulfonate group, J. Chromatogr. A 1564 (2018) 137-144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polarity-based fractionation in proteomics: hydrophilic interaction vs reversed-phase liquid chromatography, Biomed

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Jafari, M. Mirzaie, M. Khodabandeh, H. Rezadoost, A. Ghassempour, H.Y. Aboul-Enein, Polarity-based fractionation in proteomics: hydrophilic interaction vs reversed-phase liquid chromatography, Biomed. Chromatogr. 30 (2016) 1036-1041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Studying the effect of storage conditions on the metabolite content of red wine using HILIC LC–MS based metabolomics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Arapitsas, A.D. Corte, H. Gika, L. Narduzzi, F. Mattivi, G. Theodoridis, Studying the effect of storage conditions on the metabolite content of red wine using HILIC LC-MS based metabolomics, Food. Chem. 197 (2016) 1331-1340.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in stationary phases and understanding of retention in hydrophilic interaction chromatography. A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Jandera, P. Janas, Recent advances in stationary phases and understanding of retention in hydrophilic interaction chromatography. A review, Anal. Chim. Acta 967 (2017) 12-32.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography method development and validation for the assay of HEPES zwitterionic buffer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, B. Gevaert, N. Bracke, H. Yao, E. Wynendaele, B. De Spiegeleer, Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography method development and validation for the assay of HEPES zwitterionic buffer, J. Pharma. Biomed. Anal. 135 (2017) 227-233.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of illegal peptide drugs via HILIC-DAD-MS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Janvier, E. De Sutter, E. Wynendaele, B. De Spiegeleer, C. Vanhee, E. Deconinck, Analysis of illegal peptide drugs via HILIC-DAD-MS, Talanta 174 (2017) 562-571.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of illegal peptide drugs via HILIC-DAD-MS
